summary: I started as a summer intern for Senator Cooper in 1965 and ended up working with him in the Senate for seven years. He was a remarkable man who later became ambassador to East Germany and India, and he even asked me to accompany him on those appointments. I remember my first day vividly when he asked if I was there to visit or work, and he was kind enough to introduce me to his administrative assistant. Senator Cooper's wife, Mrs. Cooper, also involved me in her social events and garden book project, which helped me stay on beyond my internship.

Senator Cooper was appointed ambassador to India after his time in the Senate, and he asked me to go with him. This was a significant moment because it showed the trust and connection we had developed over the years. While I didn't go into detail about my experiences there, it was part of the broader diplomatic roles Senator Cooper took on after his Senate career.

After his Senate career, Senator Cooper was appointed ambassador to East Germany, and he invited me to accompany him there. This was part of his diplomatic service following his time in Washington, D.C. It was an important chapter in both his career and mine, as it extended my experience beyond the Senate to international relations.

I'm originally from Louisville, Kentucky, where my family has deep roots. My mother once ran an Irish shop called the Shamrock House downtown, and my father was an Episcopal minister with connections to local churches. I grew up on Brownborough Road, which was countryside back then but is now more developed. Louisville shaped much of my early life and family background.

My mother owned the Shamrock House, an Irish shop that she started downtown in Louisville. It was initially located near Bakery Square and later moved to a complex on Channel Width Lane. The shop was part of our family's connection to Louisville's local culture and commerce during my upbringing.

Bakery Square in Louisville was one of the locations where my mother's Irish shop, the Shamrock House, was situated. She started her business there before moving it to another nearby location. It was part of the local commercial area connected to my family's history.

My father was originally the Episcopal minister at the Church of the Advent on Baxter Avenue in Louisville, where his father had also served. This church was a significant part of my family's religious heritage, as my grandfather was a bishop. The Church of the Advent was central to my father's early ministry before he moved on to work at Saint Thomas mission.

After leaving the Church of the Advent, my father worked at Saint Thomas, which was initially a mission. He played a key role in making Saint Thomas a member of the diocese, transitioning it from a mission to an official church. This work was important to him and reflected his commitment to the Episcopal community in Louisville.

Baxter Avenue in Louisville is where the Church of the Advent was located, where my father served as an Episcopal minister. It was also the place where my grandfather had ministered, making it a significant location for my family's religious history. We lived near there during my early years.

I attended Collegiate School in New York for high school and absolutely loved it. There were fourteen of us in my class, and we have stayed in touch over the years, which is remarkable. The school was also where I first became interested in politics, partly inspired by an assembly talk from my aunt who worked for Senator Thruston Morton.

We lived out on Brownborough Road in Louisville, which was countryside when I was growing up but has since become more developed. My grandparents had a house there, and we built our home behind theirs near a cemetery. It was a peaceful rural setting that shaped my childhood environment.

John F. Kennedy was a major influence on my interest in politics during my youth. His presidency inspired many young people my age to become politically aware and engaged. I think his leadership helped spark my decision to pursue political science and eventually apply for a Senate internship.

Thruston Morton was a Senator for whom my aunt, Rose Blakely, worked, and she spoke at my high school, Collegiate, which inspired me greatly. His oral history was one of the first projects in our archive, showing his significance in Kentucky politics. His career and my aunt's connection to him helped push me toward a political path.

Fred M. Vinson was a Supreme Court Justice from Kentucky, and we did an oral history project about him early on. His legacy is part of Kentucky's political and judicial history, which I have been connected to through my archival work. He represents an important figure in the state's public service tradition.

Kentucky is my home state, where my family has deep roots in both religious and political life. My father and grandfather were Episcopal ministers here, and I attended the University of Louisville for college. Kentucky's political figures like Thruston Morton and Fred M. Vinson have also influenced my life and work.

I attended the University of Louisville after leaving Bennett College in New York, studying political science. Although I didn't graduate at that time, I eventually completed my degree later at Mount Vernon College. U of L was important in shaping my academic and political interests before I moved to Washington, D.C. for my internship.

I started college at Bennett College in Millbrook, New York, but I did not like it and left much to my parents' horror. It was a brief and unhappy experience that led me to return to Louisville and attend the University of Louisville instead. This early college experience was a turning point in my educational journey.

I eventually graduated from Mount Vernon College for Women, completing my degree later in life after starting my career. This was important because I had left college earlier to pursue my internship and work in Washington, D.C. Mount Vernon helped me formalize my education and credentials.

Rose Blakely is my aunt who worked for Senator Thruston Morton and was a big influence on me entering politics. She spoke at my high school and helped me get my first internship in Washington, D.C. Her support and connections were crucial in launching my political career.

Betsy Johnson was my friend from Louisville who came with me to Washington, D.C. for our internships in 1965. We lived together as roommates during that summer, though she returned to college at the end of the summer while I stayed on. She was an important companion during that first year in D.C.

Washington, D.C. was where I moved in 1965 for my Senate internship with Senator Cooper. I loved the city immediately; it was a whole different world full of excitement and political energy. I lived first in a basement apartment in Georgetown and later on Wisconsin Avenue, and it became my home for many years.

Georgetown was where I lived in a basement apartment during my first summer internship in Washington, D.C. My aunt helped us find the apartment near Wisconsin Avenue, and it was a great place to start my life in the city. Georgetown's charm and proximity to the Senate made it ideal for an intern.

After deciding to stay on in Washington, D.C., I moved to an apartment on Wisconsin Avenue near the cathedral. It was a more permanent place compared to the basement apartment in Georgetown where I first lived. Wisconsin Avenue became my home base during my early years working in the Senate.

On my first day as an intern for Senator Cooper, I was taken to a hearing of the Senate Agriculture Committee. I was nervous and unsure what to expect, but it was an eye-opening experience to see the Senate in action. This committee hearing marked the beginning of my real exposure to legislative work.

I worked in the Senate Press Gallery during my internship, where my job was to attend hearings and report on them. At first, I didn't fully understand the purpose of the hearings, but over time I learned a lot about the legislative process. The Press Gallery was a unique vantage point for observing Senate activities.

Frank Greer was the elevator operator I went out with during my time as an intern in Washington, D.C. He worked near the old Senate Office Building and had a fabulous office. Frank was a memorable part of my social life during that summer in 1965.

The Old Senate Office Building was where I spent much of my internship time, including attending committee hearings and working in the Press Gallery. It was also near where Frank Greer, the elevator operator I knew, worked. The building was central to my daily life in Washington during those years.
